findings.claim: ADR 0040, 0041, and 0043 form a recurring-revision set: each records an attempt at the same unresolved primitive — how to measure/correct connect-mode seam quality (per-pair spacing/rhythm) at build time when the defect only exists once glyphs are assembled — and each parks or defers the work rather than resolving it (a fourth record, ADR 0044, later ships a resolution, but 0040/0041/0043 are themselves three successive parkings of the same open question before that resolution).

citations.quote:
PARK the milestone. Five build-time refinement approaches were each built and measured
against the corpus; all five fail for ONE root reason

The real fix, if a flashy hand ever becomes common in the field, is an
**assembled-glyph feedback pass**: build the font, measure the ASSEMBLED glyph geometry

PARK the dense-body kern. Two implementations were built and measured; both fail,
and professional practice (research on file, claude.ai) explains why in one line:
**even rhythm in a connected script comes from consistent connector PLACEMENT, not
from kerning the connecting pairs.**

Scoped as its own milestone in
`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-01-connection-point-spec.md`.

The Phase 3 route is PROVEN and CONCRETIZED: gated eye-body placement + kern deference
delivers rendered dense-body rhythm sd 26 on the field-failure hand (vs 69 shipped), with
the clean faces byte-stable by the gate. It is PARKED at one remaining problem:

findings.consequence: A reader following only these three records would see three consecutive investigations of the same open question (build-time vs. assembled measurement of connect-seam evenness) each ending in a park, with no record among them actually closing the primitive; the resolution only appears in the later ADR 0044, so these three alone should not be mistaken for closed decisions.
